summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Windows Vista's redesigned Start Menu, which allows users to type to search rather than navigate through menus, is the operating system's killer feature and a must-have upgrade. He demonstrates how pressing the Windows key and typing a few characters instantly finds programs, settings, files, and websites across multiple indexed locations. Atwood contrasts this with Windows XP's 'horrific mouse-centric cascading start menu' and argues this shift represents a fundamental change in computing interaction. He connects this to the broader 'Google era' where search-based navigation replaces the traditional desktop metaphor, concluding that typing is simply more efficient than pointing when dealing with the volume of content on modern computers.
key_points: Vista's Start Menu search is so transformative that it alone justifies upgrading from Windows XP on day one
Typing to navigate eliminates the need for manually configured hotkeys, shortcuts, and the Start > Run dialog
The feature requires zero configuration—it works out of the box across programs, files, settings, and web history
This UI shift acknowledges that Google's search-first paradigm was right: there's too much content for browse-and-click to work reliably
The change represents a victory of textual search over three decades of desktop GUI metaphors based on office desktop analogies
money_quotes: Vista's Start Menu lets you type what you want instead of pointing at it.
I've gone from tedious, manually defined hotkeys and shortcuts in Windows XP to simply typing what I want and letting the computer find it for me.
Best of all, I never have to take my hand off the keyboard.
The start menu has been a usability train wreck since its introduction in 1995.
There's nothing to install, nothing to configure, nothing to think about. It just works.
This new all-in-one keyboard style of navigation is unbelievably, amazingly efficient.
No more hunting for the right bunch of pixels to click; it's faster and easier to type to get to what you want instead.
key_insight: Search-based keyboard navigation is fundamentally superior to point-and-click interfaces because there's simply too much content in modern computing for visual browsing to remain efficient.
